statement.en: Antecedents and consequences of Green-tech FDI in emerging markets
statement.summary: As environmental pressures intensify, attracting sustainable foreign direct investment (FDI), particularly green-tech FDI, has become a critical pathway for emerging markets to achieve environmental sustainability. However, existing research remains fragmented, offering limited insights into why green-tech FDI enters emerging markets and how it affects regional green innovation. This thesis addresses these gaps by systematically examining both the antecedents and consequences of green-tech FDI through three empirical studies using regional data from China. Study 1 and Study 2 focus on the antecedents of green-tech FDI. Study 1 examines how host-country conditions can attract green-tech FDI, highlighting the role of environmental management information disclosure (EMID) by domestic firms in

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: The thesis presents empirical research (Study 1-3) on green-tech FDI mechanisms in emerging markets using regional Chinese data. Claims about FDI antecedents and innovation consequences are provisionally evidence-based but not yet validated through independent replication or peer review (dated 2026-04-25, appears to be preprint/dissertation stage). The causal mechanisms posited are theoretically grounded but remain subject to methodological scrutiny and external validation.

ramanujanFormula.interpretation: 緑の資本は、透明性と圧力の積を制度の硬さで割った土壌に降り注ぐ。
